sentences.text: I've got it here.
Yes, yes, [INAUDIBLE].
I try to [? sit it, ?] because it's just broken, this chair.
I don't think you can see.
Yeah?
[INAUDIBLE]
What I am?
My name is Helen Rosner.
On birth, I was Haubenstock.
But now I-- when I married my husband, I'm Rosner.
No, no-- well, nickname, what they call me at home, you mean?
Oh, many.
Helenka, [?
Helusha, ?] [?
Helushenka, ?] oh, many.
31st of July, '24.
I will be 72 next, of this month, in July.
Cracow.
In Poland, in Poland, yeah.
Before the war, we-- oh, before the wars we were in [POLISH] 12, [POLISH] in Cracow.
What-- well, I live a very happy life up there.
I was young, and I was still going to school.
It was a flat.
It wasn't a house, it was apartment.
And I live in the second floor.
And on the corner, corner was [POLISH] Street, and my father had a shop.
And when I was going to school, [INAUDIBLE].
That was my sister.
My brother, not before the war, because my brother was in the army just before the war-- but my sister and my father, in [POLISH].
My mother died before the war.
I think in-- in '33 or '34, something like this.
I was 11, I believe, when my mother died.
But that wasn't in [POLISH].
My mother died when we live in [POLISH].
That was a time of depression, in '30-- '35, '36, something like that.
It was outskirt of Cracow.
It was cheaper out there, living.
And because my father had problems with-- financial problem, that's why we change it from Cracow to [POLISH]-- [POLISH].
But you-- that was just before the war, you ask me.
Well, that was before that, you know, that this was before that.
That wasn't before the war.
Before the war, [INAUDIBLE] I was without my mother.
But through the war, long before this.
You said after my mother-- my mother die in [POLISH].
It wasn't a telephone there, and it was no company up there.
She was very lonely, my mother, and I was a little girl.
That was [POLISH], yes.
Well, I went only to state school, you call it.
And I was-- I started my school when I was seven years old.
That was the-- I don't know why, but that's how I started.
And I never went to any kindergarten.
I never went to, to meet any other children, just right from home to school.
And, well, it was a Catholic school, St.
[POLISH], name.
And was only three Jewish girl in the class.
The whole school, maybe, have only 14 Jewish girls.
It was a girls' school, very elegant.
That was a nice school, because you know, Cracow got a part of Jewish suburb, Kazimierz.
But we live just opposite, with the-- all Polish crowd around us.
And I didn't know much about my Jewishness.
[POLISH], they call me, [POLISH], which-- that mean little Jewish girl.
They knew in school.
All the girls or whoever, they wanted to please me.
Instead of Jewish, they said [POLISH].
That means sweeter.
And, but that was hurting me that time, when I was girl.
It was hurting me.
Especially when was before holiday, Christmas, or Easter, when we came to school-- when, when I started, first time I remember the girl start to cross themselves.
And I didn't know what that mean, and I tried to just, just come-- do the same.
And then I find out that I shouldn't do this, because I'm Jewish.
But I knew all the prayers, because they pray every morning.
And especially before Easter, when was this about crucified Jesus and Jesus.
I remember one song, just from the back of Jesus, blood comes.
Everybody looks at me, that I-- and I was-- because I was Jewish.
I do remember this.
It hurts, and I was a bit upset.
But we have Jewish instruction once a month-- once a week.
In the school, but in the different class.
All these 14, or how many Jewish girl, it was a teacher.
And his name was [POLISH].
I remember he was a little, and he liked girls.
And I've been-- knew about Jewish, about Jewishness, of our history.
They didn't teach us Yiddish, no.
He was speaking in Polish, from the Bible, from the beginning of Moses, and Jewish, you know.
How it was rather the history, not the Jewish-- yes, everybody laugh at the Jewish classes.
And, well, that was that.
We knew that we are Jewish, of course.
It was kosher home.
And they have separate milk, separate milk.
And we have maid, always.
His name?
[INAUDIBLE]?
Yes, one was [?
Hanka, ?] but before this was [?
Yuska. ?] They were very primitive peasant girls, but they live with us.
And they were paid very little, like something like 20 zlotys a month.
And they had folding bed in the kitchen.
And they ate separately.
They'd eat in the kitchen.
It was-- life wasn't easy at that time.
I mean, we'd been middle class.
But still, you buy-- you buy butter from peasant woman in the marketplace.
It wasn't in packet like this.
And milk, you have to go and measure milk to the pot and boil the milk, because it was going-- it wasn't pasteurized or anything like that.
And everything, we buy, buy in the market.
My mother went to the-- with the maid.
And the cooking was starting from morning.
Main meal was the lunchtime.
I remember my father close his shop and come for a, a lunch.
It was tablecloth, white tablecloth, and silver cutlery.
We always have our good crockery.
Every day, because finish school, 2 o'clock-- from 8:00 to 2:00.
It wasn't afternoon.
And when that was done, lunch.
After this, my father lie down to have a little rest, and then opened the shop again, 3 o'clock.
And that was this.
Every morning we might go to buy fresh bread, fresh rolls.
That was baked twice a day.
Yes, but I don't think that we did.
I don't-- oh, yes, I remember that-- yes, I remember the shochet to kill the chicken.
[INAUDIBLE] that we did.
That [? means ?] we probably did kosher meal, yeah.
But the shop was open all week, Saturday as well.
My father wasn't very religious.
He was observant.
I mean, we went-- we knew about Rosh Hashanah.
We knew about Pesach.
There was wonderful cutlery and crockery on the attic for Pesach.
It was, everything, so beautiful.
I-- I don't know why that was only for one week [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, yes, it was big evening.
My father was lying, and he got a white, something, shirt, whatever, with-- embroidered with gold.
And he was saying, [INAUDIBLE].
And because I was the younger, I asked [HEBREW].
I remember that, and, well, it was a very good evening, a very, very happy time.
The whole week, I was looking forward for that.
It was beautiful.
We always have somebody.
It was my mother's brother that time.
When I was very, very little, was my grandmother, my mother's mother.
She lives with us.
But that was-- that was not before the war.
It was a long time ago [INAUDIBLE].
That was on [POLISH] Street.
That's when I was born, yeah.
And that was always somebody with us on the Pesach.
Rosh Hashanah yes, Rosh Hashanah was-- have challah, and, and some cake, and whatever it was.
My father go to shul, to temple, and my mother, yes.
And I remember Yom Kippur.
They were fasting.
And I-- as I was little, I tried to fast [INAUDIBLE] [? it was hard. ?] I never could [? stay it ?] all day, but my brother was fasting, and my sister.
It was a big day.
Not really.
When my grandmother was alive, that when I was very little, I remember she has her own candlestick.
And my mother had candles as well, Friday night.
Yes, yes, it was Friday night, but not particularly very celebrating Shabbat.
But my mother did pray.
I remember how she covered.
Yes.
We went to a Planty.
You know, Cracow was a beautiful, beautiful city, got a park going through the city.
All around and through the city, you can go everywhere through the Planty.
Planty was a park, was Planty.
You've never been in Cracow.
Cracow is such a beautiful city, and I got such a good memory.
And I went with the maid for a walk, because we didn't have a garden.
And the ?] courtyard was just concrete.
And we went for a-- one hour, afternoon, to walk through the garden, through the Planty.
Everyone knows about the Cracow Planty.
Well, I, I remember that sometimes I went with my cousin.
I got a cousin, still-- one cousin who is still alive, in Canada, in Toronto.
And she was the only child.
Rozia-- her name was Weitzenblum, but her name of the-- is Apelowicz now.
We still talk to each other on the phone, from time to time.
That's the only cousin alive, and she lost everybody, whole family.
And then, yes, she got a special nurse, like [? nana. ?] And she was-- they were richer than we, and she had a pram for doll.
And she let me, sometimes, to go with the pram and carry-- that was very long time ago.
I was a really little girl, from school.
Well, that's all, what I remember from my childhood.
Polish, all the time.
My parents did speak Yiddish, but only when they want us not to understand.
But I didn't know, unfortunately.
It was sad for me not to know Yiddish, especially when I come to Australia.
I have such a problem with that, because I was working in a-- in a factory.
And this Jewish man couldn't understand how a Jewish girl cannot speak Yiddish.
But I didn't, and I didn't understand.
Well, I couldn't call it first, because it was continuously something there.
Like that school, that singing, that before Christmas, before-- especially before Easter, and that procession on the street-- you know what is procession?
They were walking with the-- with the mother, and with the Jesus with the crosses.
And going there, and all everybody was kneeling, and was-- and I didn't.
And then I feel that I am different.
But that I wouldn't say that were bad to me.
They didn't hurt me, anyway.
But that hurts me, because I got the feeling that is-- I'm different.
And my parents, especially my mother, when she was alive, tried to be-- tried to avoid this.
She tried to be similar to the other, to all the friends.
And I have, all around me, Catholics, because Poland was Catholic.
And we celebrate-- and they come to Easter.
They paint the eggs.
And my mother let me do it the same, and put in a basket.
Then when the girls went to church, I went with them.
My mother was very tolerant.
And once, I remember, for Christmas she bought a little tree, but my father was very cross.
I have to throw it away, [INAUDIBLE].
But I have all-- I didn't really feel that [INAUDIBLE] I am something bad about that, because I was Jewish, no.
No, not really, not till the war time.
No.
Yes, the war-- before, just before the war, [POLISH].
That's where we are live, yes.
We lived there a few months, still, when the German were there.
We were ordered to wear the arm band.
That was the first, first thing.
And it was already embarrassing, and [INAUDIBLE] on the street.
And that was so many different orders.
Every few weeks was something different that you don't-- you don't allow to go on the street.
You have to, not on the foot, but you have to walk here.
Then you're not allowed to be after 8 o'clock or 9 o'clock or something like this.
We have to be home.
So many different ordering every day, every few days And then we went--
What?
German, there were German.
And actually, not, not directly German-- straightaway, they make a police, or they make somebody Ukrainian.
And then they made Jewish policemen.
And we know about that.
That was on the-- on the wall everywhere, pinned up, [INAUDIBLE] what we have to do, what we are not allowed to do.
Mm-hmm, and then we have to go out of Cracow.
We have to leave Cracow.
And then when we were to Tonie.
What was-- was Tonie?
That was a country.
[INAUDIBLE] it's not a country, more like a farm, where we live in, like a farm house with the peasant.
On the horse and, and buggy, how you call it, yes?
We took with us all our possession, whatever.
We have pots, and pans, and, and, and, you know, that quilt or whatever, pillows.
The pillows were very big pillows.
And everything was put on that, and we went to Tonie.
And we live out there.
And then I was already maybe 16, 17.
I don't really.
I don't remember.
I know that the, the carriage was full of our-- all our possession.
Whatever we could take, we [? got. ?] I remember it was-- we had some jewelry.
But ?] my sister took care of it, you see.
She was much older.
And she was-- that time, she was like my mother, because I lost mother, as I told you, when I was 11.
When my brother come from army, because it was war already, and it was my sister, my father, his brother-- younger brother, who was never married.
That was my father's sister, which took us.
And a cousin, it was lot of us, whole group of, of Jewish people, and we live up there.
It was nice.
It was like holiday.
How long we been there?
That was very-- not very close.
Well, it was-- that time, it was far, you know?
When we go, like, from here to the city, that was already very, very far, that we, we took with us sandwiches and [INAUDIBLE].
That was a [INAUDIBLE], but it wasn't really far.
[INAUDIBLE] maybe in an hour or even less than this.
The Cracow [INAUDIBLE] everything was smaller than it's here.
Everything here is big.
Oh, that was a-- that was a [? house ?] which was really like a-- and I don't know how you call that, not a country.
Less than a country, that poor peasant, peasant house.
It didn't have roof.
It was straw, and not a floor, just clay.
But we have our quilt.
[INAUDIBLE] quilt, [INAUDIBLE] that was a big, [INAUDIBLE], and big pillows.
And my father didn't work, and it was like a holiday.
It was-- remind me of the holiday when I was very little, [INAUDIBLE] and we have many people around us, all [?
Poles ?] around us.
And music was there, and dancing, because nobody was working.
No, before?
Yes, she did, at her shop.
But when it was all this, all this come to stop, and we have to leave everything.
Before, before the war it was like a grocery shop.
It got everything.
Yes.
Near the [? main-- ?] where we live-- yes, it was in corner.
It was [POLISH]-- no, when I was born, it was [POLISH].
And [POLISH] was the other street, and it was corner of [POLISH] and [POLISH].
Didn't have any name.
I think just a surname on it.
[INAUDIBLE], a surname, I think-- that wasn't any name of that shop.
No, no, in that place, in the first beginning, I was very young.
No, I didn't work it.
Yeah, that is, that is-- Tonie is after the-- that, the beginning of the war.
And that's what you asked me before.
That was before the war.
It was a beautiful day.
That was summer, as I remember.
And I opened my eyes.
I saw the boys start to look at me, and, and it was one Polish boy.
It was-- he was son of the-- how you call it?
Master or major of the [POLISH].
[POLISH] is a word I don't know how you say in English.
And he was tall, and blond, and blue eyes.
And it was not a Jewish family who lived there.
There were some Jews who live in, in that county.
And that was five brother, and the older ones was Romek, and he was in love with me.
And I was top of the world that time.
That was most best time in my life, I might say.
And a war was there.
And-- but I didn't know much about that.
That was a few months only.
I don't know how many.
Maybe I was already 17, because '39 was still-- 16 and 1/2, nearly 17.
And I was very strong girl, and I was flirt.
And then I got flowers, and I got-- it was beautiful.
And we [INAUDIBLE] ordered to-- we have to leave this and go much further to the country, not so close to the [INAUDIBLE] or ghetto.
First time we heard that ghetto is starting to be built, ghetto, I didn't know.
What is ghetto?
But my sister already knew, and she decided, instead of going to the country, we should go to ghetto, be with Jews.
When was the first time you heard about the ghetto?
That was, well, after when we went Tonie, I think eight months or something like this.
And then we find out that we have to go.
It was just order.
What?
I think it was '41 or something like that, '41 I believe [INAUDIBLE].
Maybe eight months is all.
[LAUGHS] Well, we [? used to ?] have a good time [? nothing, ?] like, like you do on holiday in country.
I went-- that first time, they taught me how to swim.
It was a little river up there.
And I went for the river, and we had-- [INAUDIBLE].
And that Polish boy that thought I was-- [POLISH] was his name.
He was a real peasant.
And when come that harvest, he was-- with the blade, he was cutting this wheat.
It was wheat, and corn, and everything.
And that Jewish boy was so handsome.
He was flirt, and he was gorgeous.
And even my sister was laughing.
Always, they fight about me.
[LAUGHS] That was terrific time, yeah.
But it was very short time.
We go to ghetto.
Cracow-- it was in [POLISH], that again suburb of Cracow, [? right? ?] And it was already frightening for me.
It was very frightening, [INAUDIBLE].
Which?
Not, not directly to me, but we knew that come order to go to ghetto.
But I don't know who was the one.
It was order, don't know, not directly to me.
And that was [? the decision. ?] Some people did go further.
And we found out they all perished.
They went to ?] [?
Auschwitz, ?] [INAUDIBLE].
But we went to ghetto.
It was a-- oh, you want to know about ghetto?
How did you get from Tonie to the ghetto?
Again, with the horse and buggy, and something like this.
All family, some of the family-- for instance, [?
Romek ?] [POLISH], got four other brothers, they went somewhere to-- [? further. ?] They didn't go to ghetto.
Only he comes to ghetto.
Romek, ?] one of the boys-- there was five of them.
And he came to ghetto because of me.
That's what he told me.
And a few of the-- one of my uncle went.
And my father didn't want to go, I think, in the beginning.
But he went to ghetto as well.
It was such a time, indecision.
You didn't know what, what to do with themselves.
People didn't know.
At this time, they didn't know.
But my sister was insisting that we go with the others to Jewish, with Jewish people.
How old was I at the time?
Oh, my sister was 10 years older than I.
Yes, [INAUDIBLE].
My brother was seven years older.
I was [? the younger. ?] Actually, I, I shouldn't be born, because my, my mother was going for an abortion when she was pregnant with me.
That, I heard from my sister.
She told me the story.
They didn't want-- it was such a big difference.
And they just-- but somebody told her, no, have a baby.
It will be happy.
And I was born.
But I knew, all the time, [INAUDIBLE].
My father was disappointed.
He wanted to have a boy.
And when I was young, I have short hair, always.
And I was riding on a horse.
He wanted a boy.
But in the end, he find out that he got more naches from the girl than the boy.
My, my brother was a naughty boy.
Many wrong thing, many wrong thing-- my mother was always upset.
My mother was crying.
In [POLISH], when we lived, before he went to army, to join the army-- well, he-- maybe the reason, he was small like me.
[INAUDIBLE] little bit-- my sister was taller.
He was red-head.
And he got all Polish friends, my brother.
And he, he had a complex that he was Jewish.
And he did many wrong thing, want he shouldn't have done.
Kuba. ?] [?
Jacob. ?] That-- [?
Kuba, ?] we call him [?
Kuba. ?] Same as my uncle.
Sorry?
My sister, [?
Yanka, ?] Joanna, Joanna-- my sister was everything to me, really.
She brought me up, and, and when my mother died, it was such a tragedy for me.
It was such a trauma.
I was all by myself in [POLISH].
And, and that was the time-- the worst time of my life, maybe, when my mother died.
But that was before the war.
Yeah.
I didn't know how it looks, ghetto.
I know, in Tonie, that we are going to ghetto.
I didn't know what that mean.
But when we arrive, I know that was a wall, brick wall.
And it was a entrance, like a big, big entrance there.
And were Germans standing on this entrance.
And we were located in some [POLISH] Street, I think, in [POLISH].
It was huge, big, very big, big house.
Many, many family lives up there.
[INAUDIBLE] was already one couple with two girls.
He was making chocolate, that man.
And he was still doing this during the-- ghetto was like a little, little town in the beginning, in the beginning.
The shops were open, and bakery was-- normal life, only very squeezed.
We were very squeezed.
We were very uncomfortable.
But And it was hard to get food.
My father used to go out of ghetto, took of this arm band, and brought some flowers and whatever, for, for us to have it.
And it was-- that's when I met my husband, in ghetto.
Ghetto got a [? coffee land, ?] coffee, cafeteria, and was dancing and music.
OK.
